fa8cec49-9e7b-44e2-b2ce-7b6a324697f1_rev.product_description:
I would have never thought that creating a simple text prediction software is that easy. After watching one of the Veritasium videos on Markov chains I got inspired by the story how Markov himself created manual and tedios "network" based on the text of Eugene Onegin by Pushkin. I was suspicious that such a simple model with just checking last few charachers can produce text that, of course being non-sensical, seems very legit on the first glance.

So, I've spent an hour of so implementing this in C++, fed it with some English texts and I've got some really decent results, for example, looking like this:

"That wild yell seemed to come a violent puff of wind, and the door to the winding stair blew to with a shock that set the dust from the lintels flying. When I ran to push it open, I found that it was hopelessly fast. I was again a prisoner, and that if I wished it I could have no choice. The Count saw his victory in my bow, and his mastery in the trouble of my face, for he said almost joyously:, you don't count now; the Master is at hand. The attendant thinks it is some sudden form of religious mania at once might be angerous. I got a ladder myself, and crossing the wall, dropped down on the other side of a true love episode of which I know one side already...."

The C++ code is as simple as this 

Anyways, real LLMs are way more complex and they base their predicitons on words, or more preciely tokens and have multiple layers of decision makings. However, a simple code like this can still produce interesting results.

actual_review_data.review_text: The output I pasted is actually based when max_length=15, but even with smaller numbers it can look pretty good.

initial_prediction_data.review_text:
I just had a fascinating experience diving into the world of Markov chains! Inspired by a Veritasium video, I decided to implement a simple text prediction model in C++. 🖥️ It’s incredible how a straightforward approach can yield surprisingly coherent results, even if they are nonsensical at times. 

Here’s a snippet of what I generated:
"That wild yell seemed to come a violent puff of wind..."

This exercise reminded me of the foundational concepts behind more complex LLM architectures. While real models utilize layers of tokens and intricate decision-making, it’s refreshing to explore the basics and appreciate how far we’ve come in AI. 

Curious to hear your thoughts on balancing foundational knowledge with cutting-edge advancements! #AI #MachineLearning #C++ #MarkovChains

corrected_prediction_data.review_text:
I recently took a deep dive into the fascinating world of Markov chains, inspired by a Veritasium video. It’s amazing how a simple text prediction model can be implemented in C++ with just a few lines of code! 🖥️ I spent about an hour coding this up and fed it some English text, yielding surprisingly coherent results, even if they are a bit nonsensical. 

Here’s a snippet of what I generated:

I recently embarked on a hands-on journey into the world of Markov chains, inspired by a fascinating Veritasium video. I was amazed to discover how straightforward it is to create a simple text prediction model in C++. After spending about an hour coding, I fed it some English text, and the results were surprisingly coherent, albeit a bit nonsensical. 

Here’s a snippet of what I generated:
"That wild yell seemed to come a violent puff of wind..."

This experience not only reinforced my understanding of foundational concepts but also highlighted the stark contrast between these simple models and the complexities of real LLMs, which rely on layers of tokens and intricate decision-making processes. 

I believe that exploring these basics is crucial for anyone working in AI, as it enhances our ability to collaborate effectively and adapt to new challenges. How do you integrate foundational knowledge with the latest advancements in AI? I’d love to hear your thoughts! #AI #MachineLearning #C++ #MarkovChains #Collaboration
